evals.prompt: I'm building an enterprise SaaS project management platform from scratch. We have two user types: workspace admins (who set up the environment, manage permissions, invite users) and project members (who create tasks, track progress, collaborate). Help me design the full platform architecture — I need to understand the key capabilities at each layer, how the admin and member journeys interact, and where the biggest design risks are for a zero-to-one launch.
evals.expected_output: A structured platform architecture covering all 5 layers (data, logic, API, application, observability), a capability map, dual-role journey interaction analysis, and zero-to-one risk callouts.

assertions.name: covers_five_layers
assertions.description: Response explicitly addresses all five architectural layers: data, business logic, API/integration, application, and observability

assertions.name: capability_map_present
assertions.description: Response includes a capability map or table that assigns capabilities to layers

assertions.name: cross_role_dependencies
assertions.description: Response identifies where the admin journey and member journey depend on each other or create blocking points

assertions.name: zero_to_one_guidance
assertions.description: Response includes concrete zero-to-one scoping advice or a launch checklist

evals.prompt: We just launched our B2B analytics platform 3 months ago. We have about 80 enterprise customers and we're seeing good activation but poor retention — users log in during onboarding week but drop off after that. I need to design an event tracking plan and analytics framework to understand exactly where retention is breaking down. What events should we track, what dimensions should we analyze by, and how should I define our North Star Metric?
evals.expected_output: A practical event tracking plan with event names, triggers, and properties; analytics dimensions organized by category; a North Star Metric recommendation with decomposition tree; and data validation steps.

assertions.name: event_tracking_plan
assertions.description: Response includes a concrete event tracking plan with event names, triggers, and key properties

assertions.name: event_naming_convention
assertions.description: Event names follow noun_verb convention (e.g., report_viewed, dashboard_created)

assertions.name: nsm_with_decomposition
assertions.description: Response recommends a specific North Star Metric and breaks it down into acquisition, activation, retention, and expansion sub-metrics

assertions.name: retention_focus
assertions.description: The tracking plan specifically addresses the retention problem described — not just generic tracking advice

evals.prompt: I need to write a PRD for a new 'Role-Based Access Control' feature on our enterprise SaaS platform. Right now everyone on a tenant has the same permissions, but enterprise buyers keep asking for admin vs. editor vs. viewer roles before they'll sign contracts. This is blocking deals. The PM who was leading this just left the company, so I'm picking it up and need to write something the engineering team can actually work from. Can you help me write a complete PRD?
evals.expected_output: A complete PRD covering problem statement, north star connection, target users with role definitions, scoped requirements, data model impact, API changes, open questions, and success metrics.

assertions.name: complete_prd_structure
assertions.description: PRD includes all major sections: problem statement, scope, user stories or functional requirements, data model impact, and success metrics

assertions.name: testable_requirements
assertions.description: Functional requirements are specific and testable — not vague like 'users should be able to manage permissions'

assertions.name: role_definitions
assertions.description: PRD clearly defines what admin, editor, and viewer roles can and cannot do

assertions.name: data_model_addressed
assertions.description: PRD addresses how the data model needs to change to support RBAC (new entities, fields, or relationships)

assertions.name: out_of_scope_explicit
assertions.description: PRD explicitly states what is out of scope for this version

assertions.name: qa_testing_impact
assertions.description: PRD includes a section or callout specifically highlighting what QA/testing teams need to pay attention to — covering high-risk surfaces, regression scope, or specific edge cases introduced by the change
